does: This paper investigates vulnerabilities in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) that expose internal step-by-step reasoning, revealing that attack success correlates with attention patterns over harmful tokens. The authors propose an RL-based jailbreak method that optimizes adversarial prompts by incorporating attention signals directly into the reward function, guiding the model to allocate lower attention to harmful content in inputs while concentrating on it during reasoning generation. They also introduce diverse persuasion strategies to expand the action space and demonstrate improved attack success rates across multiple open-source and closed-source LRMs through systematic experiments.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining safety vulnerabilities in large language models, extending prior jailbreak research from standard LLMs to the newer class of reasoning models that generate explicit intermediate steps. It connects to prior RL-based adversarial attack methods that optimize prompts through reinforcement learning, while introducing the novel insight that attention patterns provide interpretable signals for attack design. The paper responds to recent observations that reasoning models are particularly vulnerable to jailbreaks and advances beyond earlier prompt injection and manipulation techniques by explicitly modeling the relationship between attention allocation and attack effectiveness.
enables: This research opens directions for understanding and defending against attention-based vulnerabilities in reasoning models, suggesting that attention patterns serve as a diagnostic signal for both attacks and defenses. It enables future work on attention-aware defense mechanisms that robustly constrain harmful token attention regardless of input structure, as well as more sophisticated adversarial training procedures that incorporate attention-based robustness objectives. The explicit connection between attention patterns and jailbreak success also motivates research into interpretable safety mechanisms and whether architectural modifications to attention computation could mitigate these risks, while providing a foundation for evaluating reasoning model safety more comprehensively.
